title: J. J. Abrams

infobox.value: Jeffrey Jacob Abrams   (1966-06-27) June 27, 1966 (age 59)  [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"), U.S.
* Film director * film producer * screenwriter * composer
* [Gerald W. Abrams](/wiki/Gerald_W._Abrams "Gerald W. Abrams") (father) * [Carol Ann Kelvin](/wiki/Carol_Ann_Abrams "Carol Ann Abrams") (mother)

([/ˈeɪbrəmz/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"); born June 27, 1966)[[1]](#cite_note-britannica-1) is an American [filmmaker](/wiki/Filmmaker "Filmmaker"). He is best known for his works in the genres of [action](/wiki/Action_(genre) "Action (genre)"), [drama](/wiki/Drama_(genre) "Drama (genre)"), and [science fiction](/wiki/Science_fiction_film "Science fiction film"). Abrams wrote and produced films such as *[Regarding Henry](/wiki/Regarding_Henry "Regarding Henry")* (1991), *[Forever Young](/wiki/Forever_Young_(1992_film) "Forever Young (1992 film)")* (1992), *[Armageddon](/wiki/Armageddon_(1998_film) "Armageddon (1998 film)")* (1998), *[Cloverfield](/wiki/Cloverfield "Cloverfield")* (2008), *[Star Trek](/wiki/Star_Trek_(2009_film) "Star Trek (2009 film)")* (2009), *[Super 8](/wiki/Super_8_(2011_film) "Super 8 (2011 film)")* (2011), and the *[Star Wars](/wiki/Star_Wars "Star Wars")* sequels *[The Force Awakens](/wiki/The_Force_Awakens "The Force Awakens")* (2015) and *[The Rise of Skywalker](/wiki/The_Rise_of_Skywalker "The Rise of Skywalker")* (2019). Abrams's films have grossed over $4 billion worldwide, making him the [tenth highest-grossing film director of all-time](/wiki/List_of_highest-grossing_film_directors "List of highest-grossing film directors"), not adjusted for inflation. As of 2026, Abrams is rated the 140th best movie director of all time by an IMDb aggregator of 34 rankings.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

American filmmaker (born 1966)

| J. J. Abrams | |

**Jeffrey Jacob Abrams** ([/ˈeɪbrəmz/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"); born June 27, 1966)[[1]](#cite_note-britannica-1) is an American [filmmaker](/wiki/Filmmaker "Filmmaker"). He is best known for his works in the genres of [action](/wiki/Action_(genre) "Action (genre)"), [drama](/wiki/Drama_(genre) "Drama (genre)"), and [science fiction](/wiki/Science_fiction_film "Science fiction film"). Abrams wrote and produced films such as *[Regarding Henry](/wiki/Regarding_Henry "Regarding Henry")* (1991), *[Forever Young](/wiki/Forever_Young_(1992_film) "Forever Young (1992 film)")* (1992), *[Armageddon](/wiki/Armageddon_(1998_film) "Armageddon (1998 film)")* (1998), *[Cloverfield](/wiki/Cloverfield "Cloverfield")* (2008), *[Star Trek](/wiki/Star_Trek_(2009_film) "Star Trek (2009 film)")* (2009), *[Super 8](/wiki/Super_8_(2011_film) "Super 8 (2011 film)")* (2011), and the *[Star Wars](/wiki/Star_Wars "Star Wars")* sequels *[The Force Awakens](/wiki/The_Force_Awakens "The Force Awakens")* (2015) and *[The Rise of Skywalker](/wiki/The_Rise_of_Skywalker "The Rise of Skywalker")* (2019). Abrams's films have grossed over $4 billion worldwide, making him the [tenth highest-grossing film director of all-time](/wiki/List_of_highest-grossing_film_directors "List of highest-grossing film directors"), not adjusted for inflation. As of 2026, Abrams is rated the 140th best movie director of all time by an IMDb aggregator of 34 rankings.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

Abrams has created numerous television series, including *[Felicity](/wiki/Felicity_(TV_series) "Felicity (TV series)")* (co-creator, 1998–2002), *[Alias](/wiki/Alias_(TV_series) "Alias (TV series)")* (2001–2006), *[Lost](/wiki/Lost_(TV_series) "Lost (TV series)")* (co-creator, 2004–2010), *[Fringe](/wiki/Fringe_(TV_series) "Fringe (TV series)")* (co-creator, 2008–2013), and [*Duster*](/wiki/Duster_(TV_series) "Duster (TV series)") (co-creator, 2025). He won two [Emmy Awards](/wiki/Emmy_Awards "Emmy Awards") for *Lost*: [Outstanding Directing for a Drama Series](/wiki/Outstanding_Directing_for_a_Drama_Series "Outstanding Directing for a Drama Series") and [Outstanding Drama Series](/wiki/Primetime_Emmy_Award_for_Outstanding_Drama_Series "Primetime Emmy Award for Outstanding Drama Series").

His directorial film work includes *[Mission: Impossible III](/wiki/Mission:_Impossible_III "Mission: Impossible III")* (2006), *Star Trek* (2009), *[Super 8](/wiki/Super_8_(2011_film) "Super 8 (2011 film)")* (2011), and *[Star Trek Into Darkness](/wiki/Star_Trek_Into_Darkness "Star Trek Into Darkness")* (2013). He also directed, co-produced, and co-wrote *[The Force Awakens](/wiki/The_Force_Awakens "The Force Awakens")*, the seventh episode of the *[Star Wars](/wiki/Star_Wars "Star Wars")* [Skywalker Saga](/wiki/List_of_Star_Wars_films#Skywalker_Saga "List of Star Wars films") and the first film of the [sequel trilogy](/wiki/Star_Wars_sequel_trilogy "Star Wars sequel trilogy"). The film is his highest grossing, the [sixth-highest-grossing film of all time not adjusted for inflation](/wiki/List_of_highest-grossing_films "List of highest-grossing films"), as well as one of the [most expensive films ever made](/wiki/List_of_most_expensive_films "List of most expensive films"). He returned to *Star Wars* by executive producing *[The Last Jedi](/wiki/Star_Wars:_The_Last_Jedi "Star Wars: The Last Jedi")* (2017), and directing, co-producing, and co-writing *[The Rise of Skywalker](/wiki/Star_Wars:_The_Rise_of_Skywalker "Star Wars: The Rise of Skywalker")* (2019).[[3]](#cite_note-3)

Abrams's frequent collaborators include producer [Bryan Burk](/wiki/Bryan_Burk "Bryan Burk"); producer/directors [Damon Lindelof](/wiki/Damon_Lindelof "Damon Lindelof") and [Tommy Gormley](/wiki/Tommy_Gormley "Tommy Gormley"); actors [Greg Grunberg](/wiki/Greg_Grunberg "Greg Grunberg"), [Simon Pegg](/wiki/Simon_Pegg "Simon Pegg"), [Amanda Foreman](/wiki/Amanda_Foreman_(actress) "Amanda Foreman (actress)"), and [Keri Russell](/wiki/Keri_Russell "Keri Russell"); composers [Michael Giacchino](/wiki/Michael_Giacchino "Michael Giacchino") and [John Williams](/wiki/John_Williams "John Williams"); writers [Alex Kurtzman](/wiki/Alex_Kurtzman "Alex Kurtzman") and [Roberto Orci](/wiki/Roberto_Orci "Roberto Orci"), cinematographers [Dan Mindel](/wiki/Dan_Mindel "Dan Mindel") and [Larry Fong](/wiki/Larry_Fong "Larry Fong"); and editors [Maryann Brandon](/wiki/Maryann_Brandon "Maryann Brandon") and [Mary Jo Markey](/wiki/Mary_Jo_Markey "Mary Jo Markey").[*[not verified in body](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Early life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=J._J._Abrams&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life")]

Abrams was born on June 27, 1966, in [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"), to veteran television producer [Gerald W. Abrams](/wiki/Gerald_W._Abrams "Gerald W. Abrams") (born 1939) of [Polish-Jewish](/wiki/History_of_the_Jews_in_Poland "History of the Jews in Poland") descent and [Carol Ann Abrams](/wiki/Carol_Ann_Abrams "Carol Ann Abrams") (née Kelvin; 1942–2012), a [Peabody Award](/wiki/Peabody_Awards "Peabody Awards") winning television executive producer as well as author and law academic.[[4]](#cite_note-variety-4) His sister is the screenwriter Tracy Rosen. His father worked at [CBS](/wiki/CBS "CBS") in [Midtown Manhattan](/wiki/Midtown_Manhattan "Midtown Manhattan") the year prior to Abrams's birth. By 1971, the family had relocated to Los Angeles. His mother worked as a real estate agent while Abrams and his sister were at school.[[4]](#cite_note-variety-4)

Abrams attended [Palisades High School](/wiki/Palisades_Charter_High_School "Palisades Charter High School") and after graduation planned on going to art school rather than a traditional college but eventually enrolled at [Sarah Lawrence College](/wiki/Sarah_Lawrence_College "Sarah Lawrence College"), in [Bronxville](/wiki/Bronxville,_New_York "Bronxville, New York"), New York.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

## Career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=J._J._Abrams&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Career")]

### Early career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=J._J._Abrams&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Early career")]

Abrams's first job in the movie business was at age 16 when he wrote the music for [Don Dohler](/wiki/Don_Dohler "Don Dohler")'s 1982 horror movie *[Nightbeast](/wiki/Nightbeast "Nightbeast")*. During his senior year at college, he teamed with Jill Mazursky, the daughter of award-winning writer/director [Paul Mazursky](/wiki/Paul_Mazursky "Paul Mazursky"), to write a feature [film treatment](/wiki/Film_treatment "Film treatment").[[6]](#cite_note-college-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7) Purchased by [Touchstone Pictures](/wiki/Touchstone_Pictures "Touchstone Pictures"), the treatment was the basis for *[Taking Care of Business](/wiki/Taking_Care_of_Business_(film) "Taking Care of Business (film)")*, Abrams's first produced film, which starred [Charles Grodin](/wiki/Charles_Grodin "Charles Grodin") and [James Belushi](/wiki/James_Belushi "James Belushi") and was directed by [Academy Award](/wiki/Academy_Awards "Academy Awards") winner [Arthur Hiller](/wiki/Arthur_Hiller "Arthur Hiller"). He followed with *[Regarding Henry](/wiki/Regarding_Henry "Regarding Henry")*, starring [Harrison Ford](/wiki/Harrison_Ford "Harrison Ford"), and *[Forever Young](/wiki/Forever_Young_(1992_film) "Forever Young (1992 film)")*, starring [Mel Gibson](/wiki/Mel_Gibson "Mel Gibson"). He also co-wrote with Mazursky the script for the comedy *[Gone Fishin'](/wiki/Gone_Fishin%27_(film) "Gone Fishin' (film)")* starring [Joe Pesci](/wiki/Joe_Pesci "Joe Pesci") and [Danny Glover](/wiki/Danny_Glover "Danny Glover").

In 1994, he was part of the "Propellerheads" with [Rob Letterman](/wiki/Rob_Letterman "Rob Letterman"), Loren Soman, and Andy Waisler. The Propellerheads were a group of Sarah Lawrence alums experimenting with [computer animation](/wiki/Computer_animation "Computer animation") technology. They were contracted by [Jeffrey Katzenberg](/wiki/Jeffrey_Katzenberg "Jeffrey Katzenberg") to develop animation for the film *[Shrek](/wiki/Shrek "Shrek")*.[[8]](#cite_note-8) Abrams worked on the screenplay for the 1998 film *[Armageddon](/wiki/Armageddon_(1998_film) "Armageddon (1998 film)")* with producer [Jerry Bruckheimer](/wiki/Jerry_Bruckheimer "Jerry Bruckheimer") and director [Michael Bay](/wiki/Michael_Bay "Michael Bay"). That same year, he made his first foray into television with *[Felicity](/wiki/Felicity_(TV_series) "Felicity (TV series)")*, which ran for four seasons on [The WB Network](/wiki/The_WB_Network "The WB Network"), serving as the series' co-creator (with [Matt Reeves](/wiki/Matt_Reeves "Matt Reeves")) and executive producer. He also composed its opening [theme music](/wiki/Theme_music "Theme music").

### 2000s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=J._J._Abrams&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 2000s")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/41/J.J._Abrams_by_David_Shankbone.jpg/250px-J.J._Abrams_by_David_Shankbone.jpg)](/wiki/File:J.J._Abrams_by_David_Shankbone.jpg)

Abrams at the 2010 [Time 100 Gala](/wiki/Time_100 "Time 100") in Manhattan

Under his production company, [Bad Robot](/wiki/Bad_Robot "Bad Robot"), which he founded with [Bryan Burk](/wiki/Bryan_Burk "Bryan Burk") in 2001,[[9]](#cite_note-9) Abrams created and executive-produced [ABC](/wiki/American_Broadcasting_Company "American Broadcasting Company")'s *[Alias](/wiki/Alias_(TV_series) "Alias (TV series)")* and is co-creator (along with [Damon Lindelof](/wiki/Damon_Lindelof "Damon Lindelof") and [Jeffrey Lieber](/wiki/Jeffrey_Lieber "Jeffrey Lieber")) and was executive producer of *[Lost](/wiki/Lost_(TV_series) "Lost (TV series)").* As with *Felicity,* Abrams also composed the opening [theme music](/wiki/Theme_music "Theme music") for *Alias* and *Lost*. Abrams directed and wrote the two-part pilot for *Lost* and remained active producer for the first half of the season. Also in 2001, Abrams co-wrote and produced the horror-thriller *[Joy Ride](/wiki/Joy_Ride_(2001_film) "Joy Ride (2001 film)")*.[[10]](#cite_note-Neil-10) In 2006, he served as executive producer of *[What About Brian](/wiki/What_About_Brian "What About Brian")* and *[Six Degrees](/wiki/Six_Degrees_(TV_series) "Six Degrees (TV series)")*, also on ABC. He also co-wrote the teleplay for *Lost*'s third-season premiere "[A Tale of Two Cities](/wiki/A_Tale_of_Two_Cities_(Lost) "A Tale of Two Cities (Lost)")" and the same year, he made his feature directorial debut with *[Mission: Impossible III](/wiki/Mission:_Impossible_III "Mission: Impossible III")*, starring [Tom Cruise](/wiki/Tom_Cruise "Tom Cruise"). Abrams spoke at the [TED](/wiki/TED_(conference) "TED (conference)") conference in 2007.[[11]](#cite_note-11)[*[importance?](/wiki/Wikipedia:What_Wikipedia_is_not#Encyclopedic_content "Wikipedia:What Wikipedia is not")*]

In 2008, Abrams produced the [monster movie](/wiki/Monster_movie "Monster movie") *[Cloverfield](/wiki/Cloverfield "Cloverfield")*, which Matt Reeves directed.[[12]](#cite_note-12) In 2009, he directed the [science fiction film](/wiki/Science_fiction_film "Science fiction film") *[Star Trek](/wiki/Star_Trek_(2009_film) "Star Trek (2009 film)")*,[[13]](#cite_note-13) which he produced with *Lost* co-creator Damon Lindelof. While it was speculated that they would be writing and producing an adaptation of [Stephen King](/wiki/Stephen_King "Stephen King")'s *[The Dark Tower](/wiki/The_Dark_Tower_(series) "The Dark Tower (series)")* series of novels, they publicly stated in November 2009 that they were no longer looking to take on that project.[[14]](#cite_note-cf2-14) In 2008, Abrams co-created, executive produced, and co-wrote (along with [Roberto Orci](/wiki/Roberto_Orci "Roberto Orci") and [Alex Kurtzman](/wiki/Alex_Kurtzman "Alex Kurtzman")) the [FOX](/wiki/Fox_Broadcasting_Network "Fox Broadcasting Network") science fiction series *[Fringe](/wiki/Fringe_(TV_series) "Fringe (TV series)")*, for which he also composed the theme music. He was featured in the [2009 MTV Movie Awards](/wiki/2009_MTV_Movie_Awards "2009 MTV Movie Awards") 1980s-style digital short "[Cool Guys Don't Look at Explosions](/wiki/Cool_Guys_Don%27t_Look_at_Explosions "Cool Guys Don't Look at Explosions")", with [Andy Samberg](/wiki/Andy_Samberg "Andy Samberg") and [Will Ferrell](/wiki/Will_Ferrell "Will Ferrell"), in which he plays a keyboard solo. [NBC](/wiki/NBC "NBC") picked up Abrams's *[Undercovers](/wiki/Undercovers_(TV_series) "Undercovers (TV series)")* as its first new drama series for the 2010–11 season.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

### 2010s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=J._J._Abrams&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 2010s")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d5/J.J._Abrams_by_Gage_Skidmore.jpg/250px-J.J._Abrams_by_Gage_Skidmore.jpg)](/wiki/File:J.J._Abrams_by_Gage_Skidmore.jpg)

Abrams speaking at [San Diego Comic-Con](/wiki/San_Diego_Comic-Con "San Diego Comic-Con") in mid-2010

Abrams wrote and directed the [Paramount](/wiki/Paramount_Pictures "Paramount Pictures") science fiction thriller *[Super 8](/wiki/Super_8_(2011_film) "Super 8 (2011 film)")*, starring [Joel Courtney](/wiki/Joel_Courtney "Joel Courtney") and [Elle Fanning](/wiki/Elle_Fanning "Elle Fanning"), while co-producing with [Steven Spielberg](/wiki/Steven_Spielberg "Steven Spielberg") and Bryan Burk; it was released on June 10, 2011.[[16]](#cite_note-super8confirmed-16)

Abrams directed the sequel to *Star Trek*, *[Star Trek Into Darkness](/wiki/Star_Trek_Into_Darkness "Star Trek Into Darkness")*, released in May 2013.[[17]](#cite_note-17) The film was interpreted as a loose [remake](/wiki/Remake "Remake") of *[Star Trek II: The Wrath of Khan](/wiki/Star_Trek_II:_The_Wrath_of_Khan "Star Trek II: The Wrath of Khan")*.[[18]](#cite_note-18) Critics generally reacted positively to the film, while [Nicholas Meyer](/wiki/Nicholas_Meyer "Nicholas Meyer"), the director of *The Wrath of Khan*, called it a "gimmick".[[19]](#cite_note-19) Abrams was criticized for the film's treatment of classic villain [Khan Noonien Singh](/wiki/Khan_Noonien_Singh "Khan Noonien Singh") ([Benedict Cumberbatch](/wiki/Benedict_Cumberbatch "Benedict Cumberbatch")). Many felt that much of the character, originally played by [Mexican](/wiki/Mexico "Mexico") actor [Ricardo Montalbán](/wiki/Ricardo_Montalb%C3%A1n "Ricardo Montalbán"), had been lost, especially his ethnic identity.[[a]](#cite_note-21) Two years after the film's release, Abrams said of the film, "there were certain things I was unsure of. ... Any movie ... has a fundamental conversation happening during it. And [for *Into Darkness*,] I didn't have it... [The weakness of the plot] was not anyone's fault but mine. ... [The script] was a little bit of a collection of scenes that were written by my friends ... And yet, I found myself frustrated by my choices, and unable to hang my hat on an undeniable thread of the main story. So then I found myself on that movie basically tap-dancing as well as I could to try and make the sequences as entertaining as possible. ... I would never say that I don't think that the movie ended up working. But I feel like it didn't work as well as it could have, had I made some better decisions before we started shooting."[[21]](#cite_note-22)

On January 25, 2013, [Disney](/wiki/The_Walt_Disney_Studios_(production_company) "The Walt Disney Studios (production company)") and [Lucasfilm](/wiki/Lucasfilm "Lucasfilm") introduced Abrams as director and producer of *[Star Wars: The Force Awakens](/wiki/Star_Wars:_The_Force_Awakens "Star Wars: The Force Awakens")*, the seventh entry in the *[Star Wars](/wiki/Star_Wars "Star Wars")* film saga,[[22]](#cite_note-23) with Bryan Burk and Bad Robot producing the film.[[23]](#cite_note-24) Following this news, speculation arose as to Abrams's future with Paramount Pictures, under which he had released all of his previous feature work as a director, and which had a first-look deal with his company, Bad Robot. Paramount vice-chairman Rob Moore stated that Abrams would continue to have a hand in the *Star Trek* and *Mission: Impossible* franchises going forward.[[24]](#cite_note-25) On October 29, 2013, *[S.](/wiki/S._(Dorst_novel) "S. (Dorst novel)")*, a novel written by [Doug Dorst](/wiki/Doug_Dorst "Doug Dorst") based on a concept by Abrams, was released.[[25]](#cite_note-26)

Abrams directed, produced, and co-wrote the screenplay for *The Force Awakens*,[[26]](#cite_note-27) which opened in theaters on December 18, 2015.[[27]](#cite_note-28)[[28]](#cite_note-29) Despite its strong box office performance and positive reviews, the film was considered by some, including *Star Wars* creator [George Lucas](/wiki/George_Lucas "George Lucas"), to be too similar to [the original 1977 film](/wiki/Star_Wars_(film) "Star Wars (film)").[[29]](#cite_note-30)[[30]](#cite_note-31) In 2016, Abrams responded towards these complaints, stating: "What was important for me was introducing brand new characters using relationships that were embracing the history that we know to tell a story that is new — to go backwards to go forwards".[[31]](#cite_note-32)[[b]](#cite_note-34)

Abrams returned as producer for *[Star Trek Beyond](/wiki/Star_Trek_Beyond "Star Trek Beyond")*, released in 2016. In 2016, he also produced *[The Cloverfield Paradox](/wiki/The_Cloverfield_Paradox "The Cloverfield Paradox")*, a sequel to *[10 Cloverfield Lane](/wiki/10_Cloverfield_Lane "10 Cloverfield Lane")*, which was released on [Netflix](/wiki/Netflix "Netflix") in February 2018.[[33]](#cite_note-35)[[34]](#cite_note-sb-36) Also in 2018, Abrams produced *[Overlord](/wiki/Overlord_(2018_film) "Overlord (2018 film)")*, a horror film set behind German enemy lines in [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") and directed by [Julius Avery](/wiki/Julius_Avery "Julius Avery").[[35]](#cite_note-37) Abrams also produced the fourth, fifth, and sixth *Mission: Impossible* films.[[36]](#cite_note-38)

In September 2017, Abrams returned to direct and co-write *[Star Wars: The Rise of Skywalker](/wiki/Star_Wars:_The_Rise_of_Skywalker "Star Wars: The Rise of Skywalker")* with [Chris Terrio](/wiki/Chris_Terrio "Chris Terrio").[[37]](#cite_note-39) The film was released in December 2019 and received mixed reviews from critics and fans,[[38]](#cite_note-40)[[39]](#cite_note-41) while audience reactions were also mixed.[[40]](#cite_note-42)[[41]](#cite_note-43)

In September 2019, Abrams and his Bad Robot company signed a $250 million five-year deal with [WarnerMedia](/wiki/WarnerMedia "WarnerMedia"), including [HBO](/wiki/HBO "HBO") and [Warner Bros. Pictures](/wiki/Warner_Bros._Pictures "Warner Bros. Pictures").[[42]](#cite_note-44) In 2019, Abrams made his debut as a writer for [Marvel Comics](/wiki/Marvel_Comics "Marvel Comics"), co-authoring the company's title [Spider-Man](/wiki/Spider-Man "Spider-Man") from September of that year with his son Henry.[[43]](#cite_note-45) The first issue of the comic includes the death of [Mary Jane Watson](/wiki/Mary_Jane_Watson "Mary Jane Watson"), and a twelve-year time shift, with the series' protagonist being Ben Parker, son of Peter Parker and Mary Jane.[[44]](#cite_note-46)

### 2020s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=J._J._Abrams&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 2020s")]

In April 2020, it was announced that Abrams would be developing three new shows for [HBO Max](/wiki/HBO_Max "HBO Max"): *[Duster](/wiki/Duster_(TV_series) "Duster (TV series)")*, *[Overlook](/wiki/The_Shining_(novel)#Adaptations "The Shining (novel)")*, and *[Justice League Dark](/wiki/Justice_League_Dark#Television "Justice League Dark")*.[[45]](#cite_note-47) By July 2025, *[Duster](/wiki/Duster_(TV_series) "Duster (TV series)")* had been canceled after one season.

Abrams was one of the producers of an animated short film of *[The Boy, the Mole, the Fox and the Horse](/wiki/The_Boy,_the_Mole,_the_Fox_and_the_Horse "The Boy, the Mole, the Fox and the Horse")*, shown on [BBC One](/wiki/BBC_One "BBC One") and [BBC iPlayer](/wiki/BBC_iPlayer "BBC iPlayer") at Christmas 2022.[[46]](#cite_note-48)

Abrams served as executive producer and co-creator of a new [Batman](/wiki/Batman "Batman") animated series titled *[Batman: Caped Crusader](/wiki/Batman:_Caped_Crusader "Batman: Caped Crusader")* alongside [Matt Reeves](/wiki/Matt_Reeves "Matt Reeves") and [Bruce Timm](/wiki/Bruce_Timm "Bruce Timm").[[47]](#cite_note-49) The production team created eleven episodes which premiered August 1, 2024.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

On May 8, 2024, it was announced that Abrams would write and direct an untitled new film, with [Glen Powell](/wiki/Glen_Powell "Glen Powell") in early talks to star in the project with Bad Robot signing on as the production company.[[48]](#cite_note-50)[[49]](#cite_note-51) The film, eventually titled *[The Great Beyond](/wiki/The_Great_Beyond_(film) "The Great Beyond (film)")*, is set to be released in theatres on November 13, 2026, by Warner Bros.[[50]](#cite_note-52) Bad Robot's five-year deal with Warner Bros. was also extended in August 2024, although it was expected to be less expensive than the previous agreement with future projects having significant budget cuts.[[51]](#cite_note-:0-53)

Future projects include *[The End of Oak Street](/wiki/The_End_of_Oak_Street "The End of Oak Street")*, a *[Hot Wheels](/wiki/Hot_Wheels "Hot Wheels")* film adaptation, and an animated film based on [Dr. Seuss](/wiki/Dr._Seuss "Dr. Seuss")' *[Oh, the Places You'll Go!](/wiki/Oh,_the_Places_You%27ll_Go! "Oh, the Places You'll Go!")*.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Bad Robot

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=J._J._Abrams&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Bad Robot")]

Main article: [Bad Robot](/wiki/Bad_Robot "Bad Robot")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c1/1221olympicblvd.jpg/250px-1221olympicblvd.jpg)](/wiki/File:1221olympicblvd.jpg)

Bad Robot's Santa Monica headquarters

In 2001, Abrams founded his own production company, [Bad Robot](/wiki/Bad_Robot "Bad Robot"), in association with [Paramount Pictures](/wiki/Paramount_Pictures "Paramount Pictures") and [Warner Bros. Pictures](/wiki/Warner_Bros._Pictures "Warner Bros. Pictures"). Initially a television production company under [Touchstone Television](/wiki/Touchstone_Television "Touchstone Television"), Bad Robot would branch out into film production, with the first movie to be produced under the Bad Robot name being *[Joy Ride](/wiki/Joy_Ride_(2001_film) "Joy Ride (2001 film)")* (2001). Bad Robot is well known for *[Lost](/wiki/Lost_(2004_TV_series) "Lost (2004 TV series)")*, the *[Star Trek](/wiki/Star_Trek_(franchise) "Star Trek (franchise)")* [Kelvin timeline](/wiki/List_of_Star_Trek_films#Reboot_(Kelvin_timeline)_films "List of Star Trek films") films, three *[Mission: Impossible](/wiki/Mission:_Impossible_(film_series) "Mission: Impossible (film series)")* films, the [*Cloverfield* franchise](/wiki/Cloverfield_(franchise) "Cloverfield (franchise)"), and the [*Star Wars* sequel trilogy](/wiki/Star_Wars_sequel_trilogy "Star Wars sequel trilogy").

## Personal life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=J._J._Abrams&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Personal life")]

Abrams is married to public relations executive Katie McGrath and has three children.[[6]](#cite_note-college-6)[[52]](#cite_note-54) His daughter, [Gracie Abrams](/wiki/Gracie_Abrams "Gracie Abrams"), is a pop singer-songwriter.[[53]](#cite_note-55) He resides in [Pacific Palisades, Los Angeles](/wiki/Pacific_Palisades,_Los_Angeles "Pacific Palisades, Los Angeles"), California.[[54]](#cite_note-56)[[55]](#cite_note-57) He is [Jewish](/wiki/Jews "Jews") and his wife is [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church"), and he sometimes takes his children to religious services on Jewish holidays.[[56]](#cite_note-58)

Abrams serves on the Creative Council of [Represent.Us](/wiki/Represent.Us "Represent.Us"), a [nonpartisan](/wiki/Nonpartisanism "Nonpartisanism") anti-corruption organization[[57]](#cite_note-59) and the [Motion Picture & Television Fund](/wiki/Motion_Picture_%26_Television_Fund "Motion Picture & Television Fund") (MPTF) Board of Governors.[[58]](#cite_note-60) It was also reported that he attended the [2024 Democratic National Convention](/wiki/2024_Democratic_National_Convention "2024 Democratic National Convention") in Chicago.[[51]](#cite_note-:0-53)

In September 2024, Abrams signed a letter along with over 125 other Hollywood professionals urging California governor [Gavin Newsom](/wiki/Gavin_Newsom "Gavin Newsom") to sign AI safety bill [SB 1047](/wiki/SB_1047 "SB 1047").[[59]](#cite_note-61)[[60]](#cite_note-62)
